questions.text: What is the most commonly used energy device in the operating room?
questions.explanation: RF electrosurgery—most commonly the monopolar Bovie—is the predominant energy source used in the OR.
questions.submodule: SAGES Minimally Invasive Surgery Videos. Fundamentals of electrosurgery. YouTube. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=wJNt4oR-1q0. Accessed June 18, 2025.

questions.text: Why does radiofrequency electrosurgery operate at frequencies around 500,000 Hz?

answerOptions.text: A) To increase visibility on electrosurgical monitors
C) To prevent nerve and muscle depolarization
D) To allow deeper tissue penetration
E) To avoid triggering fire hazards

questions.explanation: High frequencies (~500 kHz) avoid neuromuscular stimulation seen at 50–60 Hz while still producing thermal effects.
questions.submodule: SAGES Minimally Invasive Surgery Videos. Fundamentals of electrosurgery. YouTube. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=wJNt4oR-1q0. Accessed June 18, 2025.

questions.text: Which temperature range is most associated with both tissue desiccation and coagulation?
questions.explanation: At ~60–90°C, water loss and protein denaturation drive desiccation and coagulation without vaporization.
questions.submodule: SAGES Minimally Invasive Surgery Videos. Fundamentals of electrosurgery. YouTube. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=wJNt4oR-1q0. Accessed June 18, 2025.

questions.text: What best describes the primary difference between monopolar and bipolar electrosurgery?

answerOptions.text: A) Bipolar systems have a dispersive pad
B) Monopolar devices do not pass current through tissue
C) Monopolar devices require a return electrode
D) Bipolar systems use higher voltage
E) Monopolar systems are safer near delicate structures

questions.explanation: Monopolar current travels from active tip through the patient to a return pad; bipolar current flows only between the two tips of the instrument.
questions.submodule: SAGES Minimally Invasive Surgery Videos. Fundamentals of electrosurgery. YouTube. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=wJNt4oR-1q0. Accessed June 18, 2025.

questions.text: What does the ESU convert standard 60 Hz wall current into?
questions.explanation: ESUs transform 60 Hz AC to high-frequency AC (~500 kHz) to avoid neuromuscular stimulation.
questions.submodule: SAGES Minimally Invasive Surgery Videos. Fundamentals of electrosurgery. YouTube. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=wJNt4oR-1q0. Accessed June 18, 2025.

questions.text: Which of the following is the correct wattage definition used in electrosurgery?
questions.explanation: Power (watts) is energy per unit time: 1 W = 1 joule/second.
questions.submodule: SAGES Minimally Invasive Surgery Videos. Fundamentals of electrosurgery. YouTube. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=wJNt4oR-1q0. Accessed June 18, 2025.

questions.text: Which waveform characteristic is most associated with “cut” mode on an ESU?

answerOptions.text: A) Interrupted, modulated bursts of high voltage
B) Continuous sine wave with low voltage
C) Blend of sine and square waves
D) Pulsed waveform with increasing frequency
E) Square wave with constant amplitude

questions.explanation: Cut mode is a continuous, low-voltage waveform (≈100% duty cycle) that enables smooth vaporization.
questions.submodule: SAGES Minimally Invasive Surgery Videos. Fundamentals of electrosurgery. YouTube. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=wJNt4oR-1q0. Accessed June 18, 2025.

questions.text: In “coag” mode, what causes the increased risk of collateral tissue injury?

answerOptions.text: D) High-voltage bursts with a low duty cycle
E) Use of direct current instead of alternating current

questions.explanation: Coag mode’s high-voltage, low-duty-cycle bursts cause rapid desiccation, char, and greater lateral thermal spread.
questions.submodule: SAGES Minimally Invasive Surgery Videos. Fundamentals of electrosurgery. YouTube. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=wJNt4oR-1q0. Accessed June 18, 2025.

questions.text: What is a common misconception in electrosurgery terminology?

answerOptions.text: C) Electrosurgery is the same as electrocautery
D) Bipolar devices don’t conduct electricity
E) Current only flows through metal

questions.explanation: Electrocautery transfers heat via a hot probe (DC); electrosurgery uses high-frequency AC through tissue—distinct concepts often conflated.
questions.submodule: SAGES Minimally Invasive Surgery Videos. Fundamentals of electrosurgery. YouTube. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=wJNt4oR-1q0. Accessed June 18, 2025.

questions.text: What happens when intracellular temperatures exceed 100°C?

answerOptions.text: D) Intracellular water boils and vaporizes the cell

questions.explanation: Above 100°C, intracellular water boils; steam formation disrupts cells—this is vaporization (cutting).
questions.submodule: SAGES Minimally Invasive Surgery Videos. Fundamentals of electrosurgery. YouTube. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=wJNt4oR-1q0. Accessed June 18, 2025.

questions.text: In modern ESUs, why is “grounding” the patient an inaccurate term?

answerOptions.text: A) The ESU only uses battery power
B) Modern systems do not require electricity through the body
C) They use direct current without a need for ground
D) The system forms a closed circuit with a return pad
E) Modern pads block current flow altogether

questions.explanation: The circuit is completed by a dispersive return electrode; the patient is not “grounded” to earth as in legacy systems.
questions.submodule: SAGES Minimally Invasive Surgery Videos. Fundamentals of electrosurgery. YouTube. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=wJNt4oR-1q0. Accessed June 18, 2025.

questions.text: What is the purpose of blend mode?

answerOptions.text: A) It provides maximum cutting with no coagulation
B) It uses direct current to minimize heat
C) It combines intermediate duty cycles and voltage
D) It activates both monopolar and bipolar electrodes

questions.explanation: Blend sits between cut and coag with intermediate duty cycle/voltage to balance division and hemostasis.
questions.submodule: SAGES Minimally Invasive Surgery Videos. Fundamentals of electrosurgery. YouTube. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=wJNt4oR-1q0. Accessed June 18, 2025.

questions.text: Which tissue effect is best described as high-voltage arcing across air without contact?
questions.explanation: Fulguration is a no-contact, high-voltage arc used for superficial coagulation over wide surfaces.
questions.submodule: SAGES Minimally Invasive Surgery Videos. Fundamentals of electrosurgery. YouTube. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=wJNt4oR-1q0. Accessed June 18, 2025.

questions.text: Which tissue effect is characterized by protein denaturation and reformation?
questions.explanation: Coagulation reflects heat-driven protein denaturation and reformation (typically ~60–90°C), aiding hemostasis.
questions.submodule: SAGES Minimally Invasive Surgery Videos. Fundamentals of electrosurgery. YouTube. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=wJNt4oR-1q0. Accessed June 18, 2025.

questions.text: Why might a surgeon prefer “cut” mode over “coag” when buzzing a vessel held in forceps?

answerOptions.text: A) It is faster and produces no lateral spread
B) It produces a deeper, more homogeneous seal
E) It avoids using alternating current

questions.explanation: Cut’s continuous, lower-voltage waveform avoids rapid surface desiccation, enabling deeper, uniform sealing.
questions.submodule: SAGES Minimally Invasive Surgery Videos. Fundamentals of electrosurgery. YouTube. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=wJNt4oR-1q0. Accessed June 18, 2025.

questions.text: Which of the following temperature effects is responsible for carbonization?
questions.explanation: Carbonization occurs at very high temperatures (≈200–600°C), producing black char and sticky residue.
questions.submodule: SAGES Minimally Invasive Surgery Videos. Fundamentals of electrosurgery. YouTube. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=wJNt4oR-1q0. Accessed June 18, 2025.

questions.text: What is the main danger when desiccated tissue acts as an insulator during coagulation?

answerOptions.text: C) Deep penetration and effective hemostasis
D) Prevents current from reaching deeper tissue layers
E) Causes the ESU to short circuit

questions.explanation: Rapid surface desiccation creates a nonconductive crust that blocks deeper energy transfer and weakens hemostasis.
questions.submodule: SAGES Minimally Invasive Surgery Videos. Fundamentals of electrosurgery. YouTube. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=wJNt4oR-1q0. Accessed June 18, 2025.

questions.text: What principle do advanced bipolar vessel sealing devices (e.g., LigaSure) use?

answerOptions.text: E) Grounding pads for energy dispersion

questions.explanation: They deliver a continuous, low-voltage waveform (with feedback) to create uniform, deep seals with minimal spread.
questions.submodule: SAGES Minimally Invasive Surgery Videos. Fundamentals of electrosurgery. YouTube. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=wJNt4oR-1q0. Accessed June 18, 2025.

questions.text: Why is high-frequency current safer for use in surgery than standard wall current?

answerOptions.text: A) It penetrates tissues more quickly
B) It avoids heating tissues altogether
C) It is less visible on monitors
D) It is too fast to depolarize muscles or nerves
E) It can be reversed if needed

questions.explanation: Frequencies >100 kHz are above neuromuscular response, reducing risk of contractions or arrhythmias while allowing thermal effects.
questions.submodule: SAGES Minimally Invasive Surgery Videos. Fundamentals of electrosurgery. YouTube. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=wJNt4oR-1q0. Accessed June 18, 2025.

questions.text: What tissue condition is commonly produced by caramelization?

answerOptions.text: C) Sticky eschar on instrument tips

questions.explanation: At very high temperatures, sugars/proteins melt and char—creating sticky residue on instruments (caramelization/char).
questions.submodule: SAGES Minimally Invasive Surgery Videos. Fundamentals of electrosurgery. YouTube. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=wJNt4oR-1q0. Accessed June 18, 2025.
